data.title: Sherwin-Williams: Why Some See Downward Potential
data.description:
I've long had Sherwin-Williams (SHW) in my "sights", so to speak. This is because the company is the peer of some of the European paint and coatings companies that I cover, such as AkzoNobel (AKZOY). I'm invested (heavily) in some of its peers, but I have no position in SHW. In this article, I'll detail why that is, and what could cause me to change my mind. Furthermore, I'll investigate how justified I believe the current outlook for the company is. By this I mean the outlook we have here on SA, where the last positive article for SHW was published in mid-2024, with all articles since then being either "HOLD", "SELL", or even "STRONG SELL". It's a relatively clear-cut picture that's being presented, and it's not one I necessarily see reflected across analyst markets outside of SA.

The average SA rating is a "SELL" - meanwhile, Wall Street has it at a "BUY", and Quant is in between - currently calling it a "HOLD".

By looking at the SA-provided factor grades, we can get an overview/idea of why the ratings are the way they are - they cite a very unfavorable valuation and growth metric (also momentum), while at the same time saying that Revision and profitability is "top notch" here, with profitability/margins being at "A".

So what's the truth, and what could cause this to change?

SHW is well-known in the US - less so in Europe, I believe. In the US, it's the market's largest provider of what is known as "architectural paints", selling across 5,000+ stores at mostly a higher price point than the competition. It has, as you can see in the article picture above, its own stores, but it also sells in other stores and big-box retailers. International exposure for the company does exist, but the company is mostly NA-focused.

Let's see what we have here.

Sherwin-Williams - What upside exists in US-focused paints and coatings

Over the past 5-8 years, the company has tried to edge into more traditional sales channels, while also keeping its store appeal across the US and the world. The M&A of Valspar some years ago enabled SHW to get in big with Lowe's (LOW). SHW is a bit of a contrarian company, because it keeps adding and opening up new stores. Part of this has to do with the business model. Paints and coatings are not necessarily products you want to ship long distances to the consumer, but rather buy on-site or close by. This is especially true for contractors and renovators. SHW has also improved for it's customers, by offering things like job site deliveries, while at the same time justifying (through its product quality and service), premium product pricing.

This is reflected in the company's sales profile - more than 60% of its products are sold not to DIYers, but to professionals.

This also represents a shift from the company's history - it used to be much more DIY focused. It also markets its consumer products differently, using different brands such as Menards.

One of the biggest arguments in favor of this company is how ubiquitous it is among professionals in the US. It's one of the most-loved brands for what it is and does. While the company does come with construction and renovation market correlation (which is volatile), there is something to be said for more rental property volume across the states, which is likely to drive sales and trends upwards in the future.

The now years-old partnership with Lowe's also is one of the primary arguments as to why this company could be likely to outperform even further.

SHW is BBB rated. It generates earnings of roughly $11-$12/share and year, and it pays a meager dividend of about 0.9% at today's valuation, but with a significant market cap close to $90B. This makes, on the perspective of yield, peers like AkzoNobel more interesting - the same is true for the initial-view P/E multiple, which currently stands above 30x P/E, with competitors trading at about half that, or thereabouts.

There's a significant premium baked into the share price here, it's also what is causing the neutral views and "SELL" ratings by many analysts. This is despite the fact that SHW has, what I would also agree with, a wide moat based on its current intangible assets. The network and brand power of its paints and coatings business is so durable and likely to outperform over time (in my view), that a significant premium for the company is justified. Following the acquisition of Valspar, the company is not just the biggest in the US, it's the largest paints and coatings manufacturer on the planet. It has a market share over 11%, which is very impressive given the size and sheer scope of the markets involved.

Closest competitors are AkzoNobel in Europe, PPG Industries (PPG) in the US, and maybe Masco (MAS), though Masco is comparatively small.

The business model for paints and coatings is attractive - and it has to do with how the work is done. Typically, unlike in most construction and renovation activities, material costs for paints and coats are minor. Unlike in things like metal and woodworking/reno, they make up less than 20% of the total material bill (with most professionals also getting discounts from manufacturers and stores). This is something I know because I have friends who work in a professional painting company, but there's support for this.

This means a few things.

First off, specialist labor costs are a big part of the overall project. This means that typically painters are more particular about their materials, and can afford to be, because their profit margins are higher. Also, choosing higher-quality materials means the job can last a lot longer. Additionally, painting is a very cost-effective way of renovating or increasing the value of a property. This means attractive market dynamics - and SHW, as one of the premier providers of products, is well-positioned here.

Recent results confirm strong trends despite a volatile market. Net sales were up over 3%, with 5% EPS growth - again, the paint stores segment outperformed, confirming the notion that these physical stores are really part of what continues to make the company attractive. Demand is very good - and this is what has the company trading at such significant multiples.

Valuation for Sherwin-Williams - Attractive only if you disregard 20 years of valuation trends

Sherwin-Williams is attractive only in a very narrow and premium scenario. If you look at where the company trades right now, close to $357/share, which represents a P/E of around 31x, this comes to a 60-100% premium to most of its closer peers, including AkzoNobel, which currently trades at about 15.3x. The latter-mentioned also comes with a 3.3%+ dividend yield, more than 3x the size of SHW, and a similar credit rating as well as lower debt. The things that SHW has going for it are better forecast accuracy and a more established and secured end market.

But let's say that you accept the company's premium of 29-30x, despite knowing how outsized this is to sector averages. Your expected returns, even if the company hits its own growth targets and earnings growth forecasts, would still not even meet 9% per year.

If you're an investor that values beating the market, I believe this is not all that favorable rate of return, and should be avoided in favor of better alternatives.

It should be mentioned that my PT of around $300/share or below is actually fairly high, in the context of both sector and analyst targets. I forecast a growth of 5% (above 3-4% of from others), with a 17-18% EBIT margin, above the 14-15% year longer-term averages. My assumptions include the forecast of generating a 35-45%+ ROIC on newly invested capital. This is to be put in context to the COVID-19 renovation surge (That's DIY, not pros), which might be distorting company numbers a bit, but we also have a slowdown in the overall housing market that's affecting things here. This slowdown has had the result that both pro segments and DIY segments remain somewhat muted, with upward potential (and that's also what I am, in part, accounting for when forecasting here).

The primary risks to the company are macro-related and valuation-related - and they make the company relatively unattractive when put next to something like AkzoNobel, which also has an upside on the 3-4 year forecast from the same trend upside SHW sees, but at a substantially lower forward forecast.

So, AkzoNobel offers potential market-beating performance at a forward P/E of less than 17x. SHW doesn't offer market-beating performance, but only half of that return, at a forward P/E of close to 30x. That's an imbalance, or difference that I find impossible to reconcile as of January 2026. For these reasons, and this alternative, AkzoNobel represents a "BUY" for me, while SHW is only a "HOLD". I wouldn't necessarily bet against SHW and go "SELL", but I would never buy at this price. The risk, if not for significant underperformance, for lackluster returns is so clear to me that I would be very careful here.

Risks & Considerations

Aside from general end-market cyclicality and volatile demand correlation (though again, there's plenty of proof to suggest that the company is atypically resilient when it comes to trends like this), the risks for SHW are relatively few. The company deserves, due to its moats and upside, a certain premium to its valuation and share price, and the history and current trends of its sales, including the latest quarterly results, suggest further potential upside for the company at the very least in sales.

However, the company trades at what I view to be an unacceptably high premium despite all this. No chemical or basic materials company (which is in the end what SHW is), should trade at this significant multiple. A market-beating upside can be gotten only in the case of significant further premiumization. Even in the case of high multiples as well as double-digit growth, you're still expecting only less than 9% per year, and that's including the yield.

For that reason, I consider the risk/reward profile to be unfavorable - and I believe you're taking too much of a risk in investing in the company here.

Thesis

Sherwin-Williams is the largest paints and coatings supplier in the entire world. I believe that at the right price, this company represents a must-have. The sector in which the company operates is very attractive, as evidenced by my significant stake in AkzoNobel and similar companies.

The company's valuation is currently prohibitive. I would give SHW a fair valuation of no more than $350/share, which puts the relative PT below $300 at the very least. This would require a more than 10-15% short term drop - and I believe the recent volatility and lack of investor returns (for most of 2025) reflect this unfavorable valuation.

For this reason, I give the company a rating of only "HOLD", and would look at alternatives for a better upside.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $300/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VPU: Time For Utilities To Turn Higher (Upgrade)
data.description:
Utilities have been a mercurial corner of the U.S. stock market. It has enjoyed periods of solid outperformance and risk-adjusted returns (often linked to the AI trade), while occasional stumbles in both absolute and relative strength have left income investors perplexed. At the moment, we are in one of those soft patches.

The Vanguard Utilities Index ETF (VPU) is in a 10% drawdown from its 52-week high. I had a "Hold" rating on the fund almost two years ago. Shares have returned 46% since then, outperforming the S&P 500. I am upgrading VPU to a "Buy" today. The valuation is fair, while the technical situation actually shows signs of potential.

VPU: Now Lagging SPY & VEU (Foreign Stocks) YoY

According to the issuer, "VPU seeks to track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment return of stocks in the Utilities sector using a passively managed, full-replication strategy when possible and a sampling strategy if regulatory constraints dictate. The fund includes stocks of companies that distribute electricity, water, or gas, or that operate as independent power producers."

VPU is a large ETF with $9.8 billion in assets under management as of Jan. 22, 2026. That’s up from $6 billion at the time of my February 2024 analysis. The annual expense ratio is low at just 9 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 2.71%--about double that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has turned weak, currently a lukewarm C ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. The Risk Grade is also not the best, though that may be due to somewhat volatile performance during the 3-month decline. Overall, I would expect its beta to be below 1.

Finally, on liquidity, VPU is highly tradable, as evidenced by an average daily volume of more than 200,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of just two basis points, per Vanguard.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar has high exposure to the value style, with more than half of the allocation on the left side of the style box. There is just 5% growth exposure, while 10% of the fund is classified by Morningstar as small-cap.

The 100% U.S. equity product’s valuation is not a bargain at 17.8x earnings, but VPU’s long-term earnings growth rate has ticked up by almost three turns since my early-2024 assessment. So, the PEG ratio improved.

VPU: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

VPU is a cap-weighted ETF, so it’s key to monitor both the fundamental and technical trends among its largest holdings. Nextera (NEE) is the biggest position at more than 11%, and it, along with many other large-cap Utilities-sector companies, reports Q4 results in the coming days. So, near-term volatility could be a bit higher than average.

I’d call out that VPU paid out a record-high cumulative dividend last year.

VPU: Holdings & Dividend Information

As for seasonal trends, January and February have often produced tepid returns. The first month of the year has averaged a small gain since 2016, while February has been negative. Bullish calendar numbers have been seen from March through May.

VPU: Neutral Near-Term Seasonals

The Technical Take

With a better growth-adjusted P/E and key earnings on tap, VPU’s technical situation is intriguing. Notice in the chart below that shares are at a critical technical juncture. The $178 to $181 range has been a pivot point historically. The risk/return profile is favorable, in my view.

What’s more, the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls still control the primary trend. VPU is straddling that trend-indicator line right now, though the 50dma has acted as a near-term resistance mark.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has been in a bearish zone between 30 and 60 recently. Price-wise, VPU achieved its measured move upside price objective based on the depth of the late-2024 to April 2025 decline, and subsequent breakout.

So, the 10% correction makes sense. Now could be the time to enter the Utilities ETF or add to a position.

VPU: Shares Tag Long-Term Support, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on VPU. The PEG-ratio value is not all that expensive, while the technical situation appears to offer a long opportunity as we enter the heart of the Q4 earnings season.

data.title: 3 Monthly Dividend Payers I'm Buying Right Now
data.description:
As a well documented dividend growth investor, you may find it to be no surprise that I like and own monthly dividend paying investments. Right now there are three in particular that I am actively accumulating to grow my dividend income and retirement nest egg. What I like about these three positions is that they are diversified and provide income from multiple different sources: option writing, business development/financing, and commercial real estate.

Specifically, we are talking about the Neos S&P500 High Income ETF (SPYI), Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN), and Agree Realty Corporation (ADC). Between these three, there are almost endless sources of income and each of them have been proven to provide consistent, growing returns for their shareholders. Here are what some of the major metrics look like over the past 3-year period.

SPYI MAIN ADC Portfolio Avg.
Avg. Yield 11.02% 7.51% 4.29% 7.61%
Avg. Dividend Growth 3.05% 5.28% 3.18% 3.84%
Avg. Revenue Growth N/A 5.92% 19.39% 12.66%
Total Performance 58.54% 108.78% 11.08% 59.47%

Based on this summary, if you were to have owned these three positions over the past three years, you would have observed an average yield of 7.61% with a dividend that is growing 3.84% per year while realizing total returns of 59.47%. I'd like to analyze these numbers a bit further to understand the context. First off, the total performance of the S&P 500 (SPY) over this same time period was 80.64% so from that high level view this portfolio has underperformed the wider market. However, there is more information that is needed such as investment goals and investor total portfolio composition before a true judgment can be made about these returns.

For myself, these are not my primary and dominant investments in my portfolio and they serve a purpose in the larger picture of my investment goals. More about my strategy can be found in the latest update from dividend growth portfolio here. The goal for these monthly dividend payers has multiple purposes, chiefly being a source of new capital to invest in other high growth positions without having to inject more capital into my account, and second to provide stability to my portfolio against stocks that are more sensitive to geopolitics and macroeconomics. In combination with all of my other positions, I find myself with a nice blend of total performance, yield, dividend growth, and stability.

Neos S&P500 High Income ETF (SPYI)

Option call writing can be a magnificent way to bring additional income to one's portfolio. I do this manually with positions in my accounts and enjoy the rewards of getting some "additional yield" in my portfolio. However, there are two important things that an option writing income ETF can provide, scale and ease. The first is the bigger of the two, being able to write options against AUM of $7.30B can provide a whole lot more income than my personal assets under management as they will have a plethora of different tickers they can write calls against in batches of 100 shares. The second benefit does come with a price of 0.68% in expense ratio, but relieves me of the burden to constantly analyze, write, and manage covered call options across my accounts.

The main risks associated with a covered call ETF like this are mainly reduced yields from the covered calls as premiums shift and price depreciation if the underlying stocks depreciate. For those fully relying on the income, these two risks can throw a wrench in financial planning. For me personally, this is less of a concern as I am not anywhere near retirement and using the cash purely for other investments or to continue snowballing.

Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN)

This is one of my favorite stocks in general, not just amongst monthly dividend payers. I was first exposed to BDCs and their small to medium business loans model when I was running a startup and it opened up a whole new world of finance to me that I was not as well-versed in. Main Street capital thrives in the area of financing smaller and younger businesses at favorable rates for either debt financing or equity. Companies like MAIN are the underlying back bone of the capitalistic economy that are the somewhat unsung heroes (I'm sure many would disagree with the term "hero") of the startup ecosystem and in-turn large scale capitalism.

Main Street provides a multitude of services from mezzanine financing, growth financing, small market funding, medium market funding, minority equity investment, majority equity investment, refinancing, management buyouts, and strategic acquisition funding just to name a few. Not only that but their investments are not concentrated in any particular region, per their latest earnings presentation, they are actually pretty evenly distributed across the country.

But what I really love about this company is the consistent, growing dividend. Unique as they already are with making monthly payments, they have also increased their dividend consistently without a single reduction in monthly distributions (and yes that includes the great recession and the Covid pandemic). The cherry on top is that they consistently reward shareholders with supplemental payouts throughout the year. Like clockwork for the past three years, MAIN has paid additional quarterly special dividends bringing the annual number of payouts to 16 per year!

Risks associated with Main Street are tied more closely to the broader market, as the economy thrives, MAIN thrives. I believe Main Street has demonstrated themselves to be reliable distributors through not one but two major economic crises that we have endured in the past 20 years, giving me confidence that their fortified balance sheet can weather any storm.

Agree Realty Corporation (ADC)

Agree Realty actually first caught my eye when living in Michigan and I would drive past their headquarters on my morning commute. Out of curiosity I began following their growth and have been impressed by what they have built and continue to build. ADC's business model centers around diversified investments in high-quality retail properties. While at first glance that may not seem so appealing, upon further research it is clear that Agree is the best of breed and has built a robust business model. As seen in their latest earnings report, ADC has set themselves apart from other retail net lease peers by achieving the highest level of investment grade tenants in the industry while also being the most diversified and high upside in rent per square foot moving forward.

While the risks are obvious for a commercial real estate REIT, I find comfort in the fact they just recently received an A- issue rating with a stable outlook from Fitch ratings as well as having a low net debt to recurring EBITDA multiple. These factors materialize in the form of 167 consecutive dividends paid with consistent annual dividend increases.

Summary

Every investment must be made with a goal and purpose in mind. While an averaged portfolio of these three have not provided S&P 500 beating returns, they have provided exceptional stability and nearly 6x the amount of dividend income annually. This makes each of these companies a great investment in my broader portfolio strategy and I will continue to build my positions in each. For those looking for reliable, high income every month these companies may be a great option for you as well.

I look forward to continuing my Seeking Alpha updates and analyses!

data.title: Fulton Financial's Strong Showing Justifies More Optimism
data.description:
The last couple of days now have been a pretty volatile time for shareholders of Fulton Financial Corporation (FULT). Shares of the company popped 2.6% on January 22nd. This was after management announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, and it followed a 4.3% increase in price the day before. However, as of late afternoon on February 23rd, the stock has taken a hit, clawing back some with a decline of 3.7%. This is disappointing to say the least, especially considering how solid the financial picture of the business was during this time.

Management exceeded estimates when it came to revenue and earnings per share. The company exhibited attractive growth during this time, not only when it came to the income statement but also when it came to the balance sheet as well. Asset quality remains elevated, and the stock is priced cheaper than other comparable firms for the most part. It is with this thought in mind that I don't have a problem maintaining it as a Buy candidate.

This follows my decision, back in early October of last year, to upgrade the stock from a Hold to a Buy. And so far, that call is proving to be successful. Shares are up 11.9%, comfortably outperforming the 2.8% increase for the S&P 500. Even after that nice rise, I would argue that further upside is likely. That is why I believe that maintaining it as a Buy makes sense right now.

The Picture Keeps Getting Better

Fundamentally speaking, pretty much everything is going right when it comes to Fulton Financial Corporation. Take deposits for the most recent quarter, which would be the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, they amounted to $26.59 billion. That's above the $26.14 billion the company had in the second quarter, and it represents a nice increase over the $26.13 billion it ended 2024 with.

What's really exciting here is that this came about at a time when brokered deposits barely rose. They increased from $843.9 million at the end of 2024 to $855 million at the end of 2025. These are high-cost deposits that come from other financial institutions and, as such, are generally not as reliable and attractive as traditional deposits are. This means that the vast majority of the expansion that the company saw came from organic deposit growth. And that's the best kind of growth.

When it comes to other aspects of the balance sheet, one area of growth that we saw involved loans. The company had $23.78 billion worth at the end of 2025. That's up a bit compared to the $23.67 billion the institution had at the end of the previous fiscal year. The greatest amount of loan exposure to the institution had here involved the $9.82 billion tied up in commercial mortgage real estate loans. Another $6.67 billion was tied up in residential mortgage real estate loans. And then we had commercial and industrial loans at $4.54 billion. No other category, besides the $1.24 billion of real estate home equity loans, exceeded the $1 billion mark. The smaller categories involved real estate construction loans, consumer loans, and other miscellaneous loans.

During 2025, the management team at Fulton Financial Corporation increased its exposure to securities also. These went from $4.81 billion in 2024 to $4.83 billion in 2025. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, dropped from $1.20 billion down to $1.18 billion. You might be perplexed about where a lot of the deposits went. The increases in securities and loans aren't enough to account for them, and we obviously didn't see an increase in cash.

The answer involves debt. Management made a concerted effort to right-size its balance sheet during the 2025 fiscal year. They decreased debt sharply from $1.78 billion to $1.30 billion. The goal here is to not only reduce leverage but to do so in a way that improves profit margins. And as you will see in a moment, that is exactly what transpired.

When it comes to the income statement, things are looking up as well. Management reported net interest income for the final quarter of 2025 of $263.1 million. That's up from $236.9 million reported in the previous year. A lot of this improvement came from a decline in the company's provision for credit losses from $16.7 million to $2.9 million. However, the increase in the size of its balance sheet, as well as the overall change in its composition, was also responsible.

Net interest margin improved from 3.41% to 3.59%. One thing that certainly helped here was that the cost of deposits dropped from 2.72% to 2.32%. As interest rates come down, banks are able to reduce how much they pay depositors immediately. By comparison, most assets see reductions in net interest expense more slowly, especially when it comes to fixed assets.

Non-interest income also performed well, rising from $65.9 million to $70 million. This primarily came from increases in cash management revenue, wealth management revenue, card revenue under the consumer banking category, and overdraft revenue under consumer banking. It is worth noting that when it comes to revenue as a whole, which is defined as net interest income before the provision for credit losses plus non-interest income, management reported $340.4 million. In addition to being above the $326.3 million reported the previous year, it also exceeded analysts’ estimates by $3.5 million. This helped to push net profits up, climbing sharply from $66.1 million in 2024 to $96.4 million in 2025. This meant a surge in earnings per share from $0.36 to $0.53, ultimately allowing the company to beat analysts’ estimates by $0.03.

Using the results the company achieved for 2025 as a whole, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced relative to earnings. Typically, I like to see banks trade at a price-to-earnings ratio of 10 or lower. And what we have here is a reading of 9.3, which clears that hurdle. It also makes the company the cheapest of the five institutions that I compared it to in that chart.

It's also pretty cheap relative to these firms when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In the chart below, you can see that, on a price-to-book basis, only one of the five firms is cheaper than it is. And when it comes to the price-to-tangible book approach, it is the cheapest of the group.

We absolutely should not ignore asset quality in our evaluation of the business. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.23%. For context, I typically like to see a reading here of 1% or greater. So this is wonderful. It also happens to put the company slightly above the average, placing it above three of the five banks that I stacked it up against. The subsequent chart shows the same relative ranking, putting it above three of the five firms when it comes to return on equity. The reading of 11.69% here exceeds the 10% minimum that I look for.

Even though I am a fan of what I see here, I'm not going to say that I like everything about the institution. There is one area of improvement that needs to be achieved. And that involves credit quality. According to management, 0.58% of the company's assets were classified as non-performing during the quarter. This was admittedly down nicely from the 0.69% reported a year earlier. Meanwhile, we did see a drop in the percentage of loans that were non-performing from 0.92% to 0.76%. But I do prefer that both of these metrics be 0.50% or lower. The fact that we are trending lower, however, is wonderful. It's exactly the direction that we need.

With 2026 now well underway, one thing that should be mentioned is that management has their own thoughts about what the year has in store for the company. I am encouraged by the fact that they see mid-single-digit loan growth for this year. The company acknowledged that there are headwinds. But they believe that these are more moderate in nature. The company will counter these headwinds by hiring new bankers and by benefiting from momentum when it comes to loan obligations. For the year as a whole, the company is targeting net interest income of between $1.12 billion and $1.14 billion.

Using the midpoint of this and the midpoint of its loan loss provision estimate, we are looking at total net interest income of about $1.07 billion. This would be above the $1 billion reported for 2025. Non-interest income is being forecast at between $285 million and $300 million. This compares favorably to the $276.8 million that the company reported for 2025. And if we take the rest of management's guidance into consideration, we are likely looking at net profits of $388.8 million, which would be up from the $381.4 million reported the previous year and would translate to a forward price-to-earnings multiple of 9.1. So this is certainly good progress.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I am encouraged by what we see here. The market was right to push shares up after results came out and wrong, I believe, to pull them back down again. FULT stock is attractive from a fundamental standpoint and is attractively priced. Asset quality is high, and, even though credit quality metrics are worse than I'd prefer, they are trending better. When you add all of this together, I believe that this justifies a Buy rating.

data.title: OP Bancorp: No Cause For Concern
data.description:
We continue our earnings season coverage for regional banks here in this Q4 earnings season with results out of OP Bancorp (OPBK). As a reminder, this bank is a little unique. This is because it has a large targeted focus on Korean-American communities in select areas of the United States, but primarily in California and Texas. Of course, it does business with everyone but has deep roots in this subpopulation. The bank offers full-service branches in parts of Los Angeles and in the Dallas city area of Texas. However, they also have loan offices in select states like Colorado, Washington, and Virginia as well. The bank and the stock are chugging along. We think it's a hold overall. It is operating just fine. We continue to rate it as neutral, and shareholders continue to enjoy a decent 3.5% dividend yield. We see no cause for concern with operations here, adding to the evidence base that the economy is healthy. Let us discuss the just-reported earnings.

OP Bancorp's Q4 headline performance is strong

The bank saw deposits remain about flat and modest growth in the loan portfolio. Revenues jumped in Q4, with OP Bancorp bringing in $24.3 million. These revenues were a 14% increase year-over-year. Loan loss provisions dropped from $1.18 million in Q3 to $463,000. As we know, provisions weigh on earnings power, so this is a positive result overall to see a sequential decline. We saw a net interest margin that held up about flat and better net interest income versus Q3, so EPS was strong. Margins were basically flat at 3.25% versus Q3. One thing to point out is that we note margins are significantly lower than a few years ago. That bucks the trend from what we are seeing in the regional banking space. Another reason we are neutral on this name.

Overall, OP Bancorp's net income rose to $7.1 million, or $0.47 per share, compared to $6.7 million, or $0.45 per share, in Q3. And it was up from $5.0 million, or $0.33 per share, in the same quarter of 2024. This beat consensus expectations. Loans and deposits also show no signs of cause for concern.

We always like to look for widening deposits and loans when we look at regional banks. That said, deposits were flat at $2.26 billion from the sequential Q3's $2.23 billion, but they are up from the $2.03 billion a year ago. Loans edged up slightly to $2.20 billion from the sequential quarter's $2.13 billion and were up from the $2.03 billion a year ago. This slow and stable growth is certainly welcomed, and new loans are coming with better rates compared to history, though not as good as a year or so ago. Asset quality also reveals little cause for concern.

OP Bancorp's asset quality deteriorates

A review of the asset quality suggests we have little to worry about credit quality here, but it was mixed. Return metrics are also strong. That said, the ratio of the non-performing loans was 0.64%, and this ticked up significantly from 0.40% a year ago and up from 0.57% in the sequential Q3. This measure has trended in the wrong direction all year, so it is something we need to watch, but on an absolute basis, it still is not a reason for major concern. The allowance for loan losses was essentially flat at 1.28% of gross loans versus sequential Q3's 1.27%. In Q3 there were hardly any net charge-offs, and in fact, there was a recovery of 0.03% versus charges of 0.04% in Q3. That was positive.

OP Bancorp has historically had an excellent efficiency ratio. This ratio has largely improved over the last year. In Q4, OP Bancorp saw an efficiency ratio of 58.87, a significant improvement from 61.52% a year ago, though slightly less efficient than the 55.68% in Q3. The return on average assets and equity improved as well, which is positive. The return on average assets improved to 1.07% from 0.84% a year ago and was better than the 1.04% in Q3. The return on average equity came in at 12.56% versus 9.75% last year and improved from 12.36% in Q3. In our opinion, the bank is still performing just fine.

Final thoughts

We do not expect shares of OP Bancorp to move much higher or lower based on its performance, but performance is improving. Overall we are neutral on the name, and you do get a 3.5% yield. The bank has a history of repurchasing shares as well. Loans are in slow growth mode of late but are up over last year, and deposits are holding up. Margins are stable, and credit quality, while mixed on some metrics, continues to show us the bank is performing just fine.

data.title: EquipmentShare.com: Rental Growth At A Price
data.description:
Shares of EquipmentShare.com (EQPT) have seen 20% gains on the first day of trading (at least halfway through the session) as investors like the rapid growth and philosophy behind this rental company, which claims to be the future of construction.

While the growth and vision are fine, realistic operating margins are slim, which, combined with sizeable interest expenses, makes it so no realistic earnings are seen. Higher expectations coming out of the gate make me cautious here despite the topline sales growth displayed.

Building The Future Of Construction

EquipmentShare.com has a mission to power the next leap in construction productivity. The company aims to do this by providing an integrated, full-service construction solution. This includes equipment rental, sales, and technology to provide integrated building solutions.

The company cites that the construction industry, even though it powers the economy, is still relying on outdated tools, idled machines, and paper processes, bleeding both time and margins. While technology has transformed many industries, this has bypassed the construction industry at large.

By combining field-ready hardware with real-time data and purpose-built software, work becomes smarter, faster, safer, and more profitable. This rental business started operations a decade ago in a Y Combinator program, and in 2018 already some 20 locations were opened. The 100th location was opened during the pandemic in 2020, and by now around 375 locations are operational.

The company claims to operate in a global market measuring over $11 trillion and, in particular, a $1.2 trillion U.S. construction market. This in its turn powers an $84 billion U.S. rental business, with EquipmentShare.com already generating about $4 billion in sales. The company has become a massive business, having an original equipment value exceeding $8 billion, while employing nearly 8,000 workers across the United States.

Key in this is technology with real-time tracking allowing for access control, preventive maintenance, avoidance of theft, and dynamic pricing, just to name a few advantages.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

EquipmentShare.com aimed to sell 30.5 million shares in a preliminary price range between $23.50 and $25.50 per share, with final pricing set right at the middle end of the range. Note that all shares were sold by the business, with no proceeds going to selling shareholders.

With over 251 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded a $6.1 billion equity valuation at the offer price, as the share count excludes preferred stocks as well, about 14 million of such shares.

Given the nature of the business, the enterprise valuation is much higher as net debt ahead of the offering is seen around $3.5 billion. Factoring in the gross proceeds from the offering at three-quarters of a billion, I see pro forma net debt around $2.8 billion, for an $8.9 billion enterprise valuation. This, of course, represents a big premium to the balance sheet, which stands at $6 billion here.

The company has seen strong growth recently. The company generated $1.7 billion in sales in 2022, including about a third in equipment sales, as operating profits came in around 10% of sales. The company grew sales by nearly 50% to more than $2.5 billion in the year 2023, as revenues grew by similar percentages to nearly $3.8 billion in 2024.

These are incredible numbers and growth trajectories, albeit with equipment sales representing some 45% of total sales last year. So far so good, as operating profits actually fell from a quarter of a billion in 2023 to $218 million in 2024. This is a slight reason for criticism.

Revenues rose another 27% in the first nine months of 2025, as rental revenues rose by 30%, with equipment sales growth coming in much lower, thereby reducing the reliance on equipment sales again. Operating profits rose by 23% to $118 million, with margins seen at levels just around 4% on $2.8 billion in sales.

The fourth quarter appears to be a bit stronger, but the issue is that the annual interest bill comes in around $275 million, resulting in realistic losses. Of course, three quarters of a billion in proceeds allow for interest expenses to fall a bit, but profits are still not to be found.

The issue is that with pro forma net debt seen around $2.7 billion, leverage remains high with EBITDA seen between $660-$675 million, for a leverage ratio around 4 times.

Worse, all these expectations rose further as shares traded at $29 per share halfway through the first trading session, with the move easily adding over a billion to the market value of the firm.

What Now?

Based on a current $10 billion enterprise valuation, the company trades around 2.5 times sales, which looks rather cheap. The issue is that much of these sales are tied to equipment sales, with the business trading at nearly 5 times rental revenues. Based on EBITDA, the multiples are seen around 15 times EBITDA, as earnings are not really existing.

A partial explanation for the low margins could be the result of a lower margin profile of the business, but also by the aggressive growth strategy of the firm. Margins lag substantially behind industry peers like United Rentals (URI), Herc Holdings, and Sunbelt.

Industry leader United Rentals is valued at around $70 billion, thereby valued at nearly 5 times sales, but the differentiating factor is that United Rentals posts GAAP operating margins exceeding a quarter of sales. This compares to just single-digit operating margins reported by EquipmentShare.com.

Given the discussion above, and furthermore the negative operating leverage demonstrated upon, I am operating on the side of caution. For now I prefer to dig deeper into the performance in the coming quarters and post-IPO to learn more about this fast-growing business. For now, the current low margin profile does not leave any earnings, while some leverage is apparent, as the setup is not interesting enough yet to buy into a position here.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 255: $25,500 Allocated, $2,739 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was an interesting week as the market finished higher last Friday, then President Trump started discussing Greenland again. After the long weekend, the market had a -2% day on Tuesday as commentary out of Davos started to occur. When President Trump took the stage, he walked back some of his comments, causing the markets to rebound, and as Davos continued, the situation continued to improve. Technology started to move higher, and the markets regained their footing. 2026 has been off to a good start as we are hovering around the 6,900 level. At Davos, Secretary Lutnick discussed how he is expecting GDP growth in Q1 to exceed 5%. After reading through the numbers coming out of the Fed's economic projections and looking at where inflation and interest rates are, I believe that the economic backdrop is setting up well for equities. I am excited to continue allocating capital toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, as I feel there are many undervalued positions heading into a lower rate environment. The narrative around the American economy out of Davos was bullish, and the combination of income and value should catch a bid as rates decline. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is doing exceptionally well, and I believe it will thrive in a lower rate environment.

After allocating $25,500 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 255 at $34,444.19. The account's profitability increased (6.01%) to $8,944.19, which is a return on invested capital of 35.08%. This week, I added to my positions in Verizon (VZ) and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $52.79 of dividend income in week 255. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $18.64 (0.69%) to $2,739.40. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,758.95 of income since its inception, which is 22.76% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. Things are going well in 2026 so far, and I am trending for a strong month of dividend income. I am excited for 2026, as the combination of a lower rate environment, inflation cooling, and GDP growth should be a winning combination for equities.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has officially made two new records as the account value and profitability are at all-time highs. The account finished the week at $34,444.19, which is the highest the account has been, with a 35.08% return on invested capital. This is the largest level of profitability I have experienced to date. I started this account with $100 with the goal of documenting how I would build out an income-producing portfolio from the ground up on a budget of $100 per week. The reality is that this type of investing isn’t for everyone, as there is a large cohort of investors looking to generate as much alpha as possible. While I have many investments for generating capital appreciation, I also have many investments in income-producing assets. I intend to continue this account for another 20 years in addition to my other income investments, with a goal of generating enough income to live off without touching my actual retirement assets. The account is now producing $2,739.40 in forward dividend income, which is a 7.95% yield across the portfolio.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $903.46 (32.98%)
  • Equities $635.31 (23.19%)
  • REITs $460.91 (16.82%)
  • CEFs $4664.74 (16.97%)
  • BDCs $274.98 (10.04%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself for 30 years in the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. In 2025 I collected $2,411.27 in dividend income from 831 dividends. After three weeks in 2026, $212.24 of dividend income was produced, which is 8.8% of the total income from 2025. There have been 35 dividends generated in 2026, which is 4.21% of the dividends generated in 2025.

There was $52.79 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $70.75. This is an increase of $24.38 (52.57%) YoY. When I look at the beginning of 2026 compared to 2025, I believe that the income path is going to intensify by a large amount. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

We’re halfway through January, and I have already generated $135.28 of dividend income, which is 66.75% of the income generated last January. When I think about the current monthly average in dividend income exceeding $225, it’s going to be interesting to see what happens throughout the month. In 2025, January was the largest income-producing month until July rolled around. I have a feeling that I will get close to $250 of dividend income this month. January will set the stage for the rest of the year, and I believe there is a good chance that I can generate close to $3,000 this year in dividend income.

There are currently 40 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There's $294.99 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least one share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I'm making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I'm going to work on evening out the sectors and having them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 39.73% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 23.19% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.27% of the portfolio and generate 76.81% of the forward income. I'm working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have sectors close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

This is getting crazy again considering that Altria Group (MO) is back over 4% of the portfolio after falling into the high 3% range. I have not added to MO in two years, yet it's still the largest position in my portfolio. I think this is a reflection of being able to find value across the market and how diversifying into different assets can help offset positions that haven’t performed as well. The top 20 largest holdings have continued to even out throughout 2025, and I am excited about the fact that the portfolio is getting less top-heavy. I am happy that this has turned out to be a diversified portfolio that can protect me from fluctuations in the market.

The top 10 holdings didn’t change week over week, but several positions generated their dividends. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,773.92, and they finished the week with a value of $9,601.25. This is an ROI of $2,827.33 (41.74%). There has been $1,403.39 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 20.72% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $680.29 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.04%. These positions represent 27.87% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 24.83% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 255 Additions

In week 255, I added to Verizon (VZ) and the PIMCO Income Dynamic Fund (PDI).

Verizon (VZ)

  • I think that VZ is very undervalued here and feel that people are making too big of a deal over its debt load of $129 billion.
  • VZ is generating $50.19 billion in EBITDA and has a 2.53x long-term debt-to-EBITDA ratio, which makes me confident in their ability to pay their obligations.
  • VZ is generating $21.12 billion in FCF, and after paying the dividend, there is more than $9 billion in retained FCF to pay down debt.
  • VZ is yielding close to 7%, and I think the current valuation is a steal.

PIMCO Income Dynamic Fund (PDI)

  • PDI offers a compelling 14.62% yield, with monthly distributions, supported by a dynamic, multi-sector fixed income strategy.
  • PDI's NAV is poised for appreciation as the Fed enters a rate-cutting cycle, unlocking value from high-coupon assets acquired in a higher-rate environment.
  • PDI has a large monthly distribution, which I believe can be compounded into a large income stream at these levels.
  • I think that PDI’s portfolio isn’t given enough credit, as it was able to withstand a higher rate environment, and now that conditions are easing, I feel that it will do well in the future.

Week 256 Gameplan

Next week I am considering adding to my position in the Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO) and Enbridge (ENB).

Conclusion

I am very bullish for 2026, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio hitting new highs is expanding my conviction. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is generating $2,739.40 in forward dividend income and is at all-time highs. I believe there are many positions within the portfolio that are undervalued, and I am excited to add to these positions. Based on the Fed's projections, I feel as if the macroeconomic landscape is setting up well for the market, and we will see elevated levels of GDP growth. The amount of dividend income being generated is allowing me to increase my annualized investments by more than 50% due to reinvesting all the income, and I believe the dividend snowball effect will continue to compound. I am looking forward to seeing how 2026 unfolds. Please leave all of your comments below, as I enjoy interacting with everyone.

As a side note, I am planning on changing the time that this article is published in the future to Sunday/Monday so it isn't as backward-looking.

data.title: AT&T Q4 Earnings Preview: Absurd Combination Of 4.7% Yield And 4.4x P/Cash Ratio
data.description:
AT&T Stock Presents an Absurdity Ahead of FQ4 Earnings

My last analysis on AT&T (NYSE:T) was published on Nov 27 under the title of “AT&T: Dividend Growth Could Resume With Strong 2025 Free Cash Flow Guidance.” As stated in the title, the article was prompted by its 2025 FCF guidance update, and it gave T a buy rating.

Since then, there have been a few material changes surrounding the stock, both in terms of its share prices and also business fundamentals. These changes have led me to conclude that the market sentiment surrounding the stock has swung to an absurd degree. As a reflection, the stock is currently priced at an FWD P/Cash flow ratio of around 4.4x only, while yielding more than 4.7%. Usually, when I see such an absurd combination, my immediate response is that the dividend is not sustainable and faces a risk of an immediate cut. But after examining the latest EPS revisions for its upcoming earnings reports, this is certainly not the case, as to be detailed next.

T Stock: EPS Outlook

As seen in the next snapshot, T is scheduled to report its FQ4 2025 earnings on Jan 28, 2026. According to the latest EPS revisions, T is projected to earn $0.47 per share for FQ4 on a normalized basis, reflecting a mild decline from the $0.54 adjusted EPS it reported in FQ3. But the projected EPS still comfortably covers its current dividend payout of $0.28 per share. To wit, at this current quarterly dividend, the EPS coverage ratio is about 51.8% in the 3rd quarter and will be around 59.5% in the 4th quarter. As detailed in my last article, the coverage is also quite the same (even more so in my view) when measured in free cash flow payout ratios.

T Stock: Growth Catalysts

Looking further ahead, I see no sign of dividend safety issues either in EPS or cash flow payout ratios. T is projected to experience healthy EPS growth in the years ahead. As shown in the chart below, its EPS will most likely suffer a moderate YOY decline for full FY 2025. But a recovery is forecasted to start in FY 2026, with an EPS growth of 8.25% YOY, followed by consistent growth for the next few years. Overall, the EPS growth rates in the next 6 years are projected to be an average of 6.7% CAGR, which will not only cover its current payout with no problem but also provide plenty of room for further raises in my mind.

And I do see plenty of catalysts that can support the above growth projection. The top catalyst on my list is the growth in its fiber options and premium fiber subscribers. In Q3, T just reached a significant milestone by passing 31 million fiber locations and surpassing 10 million premium fiber subscribers, more than doubling its customer base in less than five years (see the next chart below for more historical data). Looking ahead, the pending acquisition of spectrum licenses from EchoStar and fiber assets from Lumen can further improve T’s growth potential in this key area. I expected these acquisitions to be closed in 2026 and expect the rapid deployment of the 3.45 GHz spectrum acquired from EchoStar afterward. Development on this front is what I will pay special attention to during the FQ4 ER.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

From a more global point of view, the above growth projection is also well supported by the company’s current ROCE (return on capital employed) and CAPEX expenditures. To wit, the chart shows T’s ROCE in the past decade in comparison with close peer VZ. The details are provided in an earlier article, and I will just quote the end results below:

For businesses like T, I consider the following items capital actually employed 1) Working capital, including payables, receivables, inventory, and 2) Gross Property, Plant, and Equipment. Under these assumptions, T’s ROCE has been averaging 30.3% in the past 10 years and my projection of its 2025 ROCE is about 35%.

With my projection of its CAPEX and Free Cash Flow (the focus of my last article), I expect T to be able to sustainably afford a growth RR (reinvestment rate) of around 20%. Under the parameters, my projected growth rate is ~7% (35% ROCE * 20% growth RR), actually slightly above the consensus estimates.

In terms of downside risks, I will need to monitor its debt. As shown in the final chart below, the company’s debt burden has started to rise again in the past 2 quarters. The company has aggressively deleveraged and paid down its debt since the WarnerMedia spinoff. As seen, its total liabilities decrease from more than $367 billion before the spinoff in 2021 to about $274 billion by the end of 2024. However, both long-term debt and total liability began to increase in 2025 again. In the immediate term, the deployment of the fiber spectrum acquired from EchoStar and Lumen could also face some execution risks and regulatory risks from the FCC.

But overall, my conclusion is that the downside risks are more than counterbalanced by the upside risks. To recap, this revisit to AT&T is mostly motivated by the upcoming FQ4 2025 earnings report. The latest EPS revisions and growth projections, when combined with the sizable price/valuation change since my last writing, have led me to a large skewness in its reward/risk ratio and to reiterate my buy rating.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: Strong Growth, Natural Gas Focus, AI Catalyst
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (KMI) is a leading North American pipeline operator with extensive operations in the natural gas business that achieved record results in 2025 in terms of net income and EBITDA, a key measure of operational performance for pipeline companies. Kinder Morgan especially benefited from strong demand for its natural gas products in its biggest segment, which saw 14% year-over-year growth, and which represented more than 70% of GAAP earnings. Further, Kinder Morgan is making progress in terms of lowering its leverage, which should lead to a higher quality balance sheet going forward and lead to fewer risks for investors. I like Kinder Morgan as a growth play and believe the midstream enterprise is on the brink of a major, multi-year upswing in its EBITDA that is related to growing energy needs in the data center market.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Kinder Morgan a strong buy -- A Natural Gas Gem With A 4% Yield -- due to the pipeline enterprise growing its market position in key growth areas such as the Permian Basin. In Q4'25, Kinder Morgan generated double-digit year-over-year EBITDA growth amid strong demand for energy products, especially natural gas. The economic backdrop is also strong, with the U.S. economy growing rapidly and inflation moderating to below 3%. Strong economic performance in the U.S. and high energy needs from an expanding total addressable market in the data center industry highlight potential for volume growth in Kinder Morgan’s core markets in 2026.

Kinder Morgan beat estimates for Q4'25

The pipeline company beat expectations on both the top and the bottom lines for the fourth fiscal quarter this week: Kinder Morgan reported $0.39 per-share in normalized EPS compared to an average expectation of $0.37 per-share. The revenue figure exceeded the consensus estimate as well, by $180M, as the total top line was published at $4.5B.

Kinder Morgan published exceptional performance in its core business segment of Natural Gas pipelines which is where the midstream company earned a total of $1.8B (on a non-adjusted basis), showing an increase of 30% year-over-year growth in the fourth-quarter. This increase was primarily driven by stronger demand for natural gas products and a resulting 9% Y/Y increase in transportation volumes. Another key element of Kinder Morgan's strong Q4 performance was that the company saw a 19% Y/Y increase in gathering volumes which rose to 4,513 (BBtu/d) in the fourth-quarter.

Kinder Morgan's Product Pipelines as well as terminals-related earnings also saw decent growth, driven by higher transacted volumes: Product Pipelines saw their earnings increase 3% Y/Y while terminals cashed in on a 5% Y/Y increase (on a GAAP basis). CO2-related earnings decreased 7% Y/Y to just $145M amid lower commodity prices during the quarter.

Kinder Morgan’s natural gas EBDA -- which is a proxy for core earnings and excludes the recognition of non-cash depreciation and amortization expenses -- represented a dominating 71% of the firm’s unadjusted segment earnings in the fourth-quarter. In FY 2025, the company generated $6.1B in natural gas-driven earnings, showing 13% year-over-year growth which was crucial in lifting Kinder Morgan's total net income and EBITDA to record highs in FY 2025.

In fact, natural gas-related investments account for the majority of growth projects (90%, or ~$8.4B) which means the already dominant natural gas segment will become even more important for Kinder Morgan in the future.

Deal-making opportunity in the Data Center market

AI presents a massive growth opportunity for Kinder Morgan and the midstream segment in general. Recently, we have seen that hyper-scalers are increasingly working to secure long term energy supplies, with Alphabet (GOOG) acquiring Intersect in December for $4.75B. Further, Energy Transfer has struck a deal with Oracle to supply natural gas power for its AI Data Center builds through its energy partner, VoltaGrid.

Oracle itself signed a $300B multi-year Cloud computing/AI capacity agreement with OpenAI (OPENAI) which is part of the company’s planned $850B data center buildout. Energy demand is set to soar in the years ahead, and I believe the midstream segment of the energy market is going to see a whole lot more transaction and deal making activity, as a result. Kinder Morgan, as one of the preeminent energy suppliers in North America, certainly has the capacity to expand its pipeline network and sign supply contracts with companies like Alphabet, Microsoft (MSFT) and other hyperscalers.

More than 50% of Kinder Morgan's $9.3B project/investment backlog now relates to power generation that is directly related to the Data Center industry, which some expect to be worth trillions of dollars by the end of the decade. Nvidia (NVDA), as one of the most optimistic players in the GPU industry, expects the data center market to $3.0-4.0 trillion in annual investment spending by 2030.

Strong outlook for FY 2026

The outlook that Kinder Morgan submitted for the current year hinges on high, persistent demand for natural gas throughput volumes. As explained above, natural gas is still the main driver of the pipeline operator’s earnings growth, and therefore instrumental in backing Kinder Morgan’s EBITDA guidance for FY 2026.

For the current fiscal year, the midstream enterprise has budgeted $8.65B in adjusted EBITDA, which implies 4% year-over-year. For comparison, in FY 2025, the pipeline company published 6% year-over-year growth. Kinder Morgan also plans to spend approximately $3.4B on new growth projects, but this is a preliminary estimate, and it could be widely exceeded if the pipeline company pulls off another acquisition in the Permian Basin this year… which I consider likely.

Stronger balance sheet with less debt

Kinder Morgan is a play on natural gas-driven EBITDA growth and more pipeline acquisitions, and the company could drive this growth utilizing a higher quality balance sheet than in the past. Kinder Morgan had a net-debt-to-adjusted-EBITDA ratio of 3.8X at the end of 2025 which is down from 4.0X from the year-earlier period. The guidance for FY 2026 implies a steady debt outlook, unless the midstream platform comes across new opportunistic acquisition opportunities in which case the leverage guidance may be revised.

Kinder Morgan’s valuation

As a leading midstream enterprise in the market, I am valuing the pipeline operator based on an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio which is essentially an EBITDA multiplier. This specific valuation approach ignores high depreciation expenses which tend to be inflated for midstream companies given the capital intensity of the pipeline business.

Kinder Morgan is currently trading at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 11.2X which gives the midstream company a valuation ratio right at the industry group average ratio... which is 11.1X. The industry group includes other well-performing and highly regarded midstream companies such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), MPLX (MPLX) and Enbridge (ENB).

While I see Kinder Morgan around fair valued at the industry group average, the midstream enterprise has long term EBITDA upside potential related to the development of numerous natural gas projects in order to serve the Data Center industry. Further, new pipeline acquisitions in Texas, which plays a crucial role in the Permian Basin, could further elevate the firm's distributable cash flow and EBITDA.

Risks with Kinder Morgan

The biggest risk for Kinder Morgan is a slowdown in growth in the natural gas business, simply because the segment has such outsized importance for the energy firm. Further, a trend towards higher leverage (a ~4.5X or higher net-debt-to-adjusted-EBITDA ratio) could turn me off from an investment in KMI and may result in a rating downgrade. Lastly, a serious deceleration of AI-related growth investments in the data center industry would likely lead to less optimistic EBITDA growth expectations... and potentially a lower EV-to-EBITDA ratio for Kinder Morgan's shares.

Final thoughts

Kinder Morgan submitted a strong earnings report card for the fourth fiscal quarter on Wednesday as the midstream platform benefited from record net income and EBITDA in the 2025 fiscal year, driven by exceptional performance and high demand for natural gas products. I like that Kinder Morgan has succeeded in lowering its leverage which gives the pipeline operator room to pursue opportunistic pipeline acquisitions, especially in the natural gas market which benefits from soaring demand for energy supplies to data centers. Shares of Kinder Morgan are reasonably valued given the progress the company has made operationally (growing its Q4'25 EBITDA at 10% Y/Y, lowering leverage etc) and I see a favorable investment profile for KMI in the years ahead.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: Solid Tumor Collaboration With Janux And Camzyos Expansion
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Bristol-Myers Squibb: Increased Global Breyanzi Sales Isn't Only Hematological Product Growth". In this article, I noted that the company was seeing robust growth as part of its "Growth Portfolio" of products to counter the losses incurred in its "Legacy Portfolio". Two sectors in particular were its hematology and cardiovascular products. I also mentioned that the company had reported its Q3 of 2025 earnings as well, showcasing growth in its "Growth Portfolio". Today, I want to go over three key items.

First and foremost, the company is slated to report its Q4 of 2025 earnings on February 5, 2026. Thus, in the "Financials" section I'm going to provide a preview of its earnings. Secondly, it recently announced a big collaboration agreement with Janux Therapeutics (JANX) so that it could obtain its tumor-activated technology platforms. More about this agreement below, but the point of this is so that Bristol-Myers Squibb can expand its reach into solid tumor oncology. As I noted last time, it has good growth prospects in hematology for sure, and this would be a nice addition for its oncology prospects.

Lastly, the company also achieved a major win for its phase 3 SCOUT-HCM trial, which evaluated the use of Camzyos [mavacamten] as a cardiac myosin inhibitor [CMI] for the treatment of adolescents ages 12 to < 18 with symptomatic obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy [oHCM]. The promising aspect about this is that the company is likely going to possibly be the first CMI for these adolescent oHCM patients. From what was released in Q3 of 2025 as part of its earnings, Camzyos accounted for the largest worldwide [WW] year-over-year [YoY] increase in percentage revenue growth, with 88%. The drug is already approved in both the U.S. and Europe to treat adults with symptomatic New York Heart Association [NYHA] class II-III obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy [oHCM].

The ability to expand to adolescent patients would be huge for this category. Since the last time I wrote about Bristol-Myers Squibb the stock has gained 20.87% and I had a "Strong Buy" rating on it. I'm going to maintain this rating as this company is delivering on boosting revenue for its "Growth Portfolio. For Janux Therapeutics I'm maintaining a "Hold" rating based on what I laid out in the prior Seeking Alpha article entitled "Janux Therapeutics: Downgrading To 'Hold' Rating After Latest updated phase 1 JANX007 mCRPC Data". Long story short, it can recover if it targets earlier lines of therapy for cancer patients. So not all is lost on Janux, as it still has a path forward for itself.

Janux Collaboration Deal Could Bring Forth Solid Tumor Candidates

Bristol-Myers Squibb is making significant progress as part of its Growth Portfolio, because it incorporates immuno-oncology products. What could eventually help with that is a partnership that was established with Janux Therapeutics just the other day, which was a collaboration agreement on developing a TRACTr candidate. This is where both companies will work to develop an undisclosed novel, tumor-targeted therapeutic that is capable of going after an antigen expressed on multiple types of solid tumors. The goal is to develop potent T-cell engagers utilizing Janux's Tumor Activated T-Cell Engager [TRACTr] technology platform.

The point here is that it can improve upon both safety and efficacy compared to that of other normalized T-cell engagers or TCEs [Janux Therapeutics Phase 1 Clinical Update Presentation December 1, 2025, slide #6]. The mask connection against the tumor allows cleaving to occur but keeps the healthy tissue alone. What's the point of this mask? It is put in place to limit drug exposure on health tissue and reduce cytokine release syndrome [CRS]. With the TRACTr having the final effect of quick clearance from the healthy tissue to limit systemic toxicity. This is a good partnership, because Janux knows its own technology platform well, and Bristol-Myers Squibb has expertise in developing oncology products. Plus, it is a big pharma that has enormous commercial reach and capabilities as well.

I want to preface that this is a good way for the big pharma to eventually expand its oncology portfolio, but remains a long-term goal in mind. That's because as the beginning state of this collaboration agreement, Janux is going to be responsible for moving this candidate forwards in preclinical testing and all the way up to an IND. From there, Bristol-Myers Squibb is going to take over the IND and be responsible for all subsequent studies and commercialization. That doesn't mean, though, that Janux is going to leave it completely alone. Nope, it is still going to provide its expertise to BMY to get its phase 1 on track, as help. If the TRACTr platform technology plays out well with respect to the antigen and solid tumors to be targeted, then Janux is going to be eligible to earn up to $800 million in milestone payments, beyond the $50 million upfront payment it already received as part of developing this collaboration agreement.

CAMZYOS Continues To Generate Wins As Part Of Product Advancement

Bristol-Myers Squibb has been achieving substantial growth as part of its Growth Portfolio of products. In Q3 of 2025, this entire portfolio itself achieved an 18% increase in revenues from the prior year period to $6.9 billion. This was thanks to immuno-oncology products such as Camzyos, Reblozyl, and Breyanzi. Speaking of Camzyos, this drug approved to treat adults in both the U.S. and Europe with obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy [oHCM], achieved the highest percentage revenue growth year-over-year of 89% to $296 million [Bristol-Myers Squibb Q3 2025 Results October 30, 2025, slide #10]. This drug is already performing with blockbuster status, as it is generating >$1 billion in annual sales for Bristol. The drug has room to grow because it is approved in more than 50 countries globally.

I brought up the approvals of Camzyos to note that it has been approved for adults with oHCM. However, the point here is that this big pharma might be able to expand the market it can go after. Especially since it just achieved a successful outcome from its phase 3 SCOUT-HCM trial. Before going over this late-stage study, plus any possible catalysts to stem from this program, I believe that it is important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company it could end up being. Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy [HCM] is a type of disorder where the heart muscle of a patient becomes thickened. There are two types of HCM, and they are as follows:

  • Obstructive HCM
  • Non-obstructive HCM [nHCM]

With obstructive HCM not only is the heart muscle wall thickened [hypertrophied], but there is a blockage of blood flow out of the heart. This is not the case in nHCM, but still the thickened wall makes it hard for the ventricle to fill up with blood properly. The thing about this disorder is that it can be either due to a genetic defect or that of idiopathic [no known cause] nature. Adolescents with oHCM suffer from substantial morbidity because of exercise intolerance [basically they can't exert a lot of force or perform high functioning activities]. Not only that, but the opportunity here goes back to what I noted beforehand, which is that it might be possible for Camzyos to become the first cardiac myosin inhibitor for these adolescent oHCM patients ages 12 to <18. The global hypertrophic cardiomyopathy treatment market is projected to reach $1.63 billion by 2030. This is a large market opportunity, but it is important to state that the goal is to go after those with oHCM. Even if only accounting for these HCM patients, then it would still be a very large market opportunity. From what I could find, it is said that about 70% of HCM patients suffer from the obstructive type of condition.

In order to determine whether or not Camzyos would be effective in treating these symptomatic obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy [oHCM] patients ages 12 to <18, it ran the phase 3 SCOUT-HCM study. This late-stage trial recruited up to a total of 44 patients who were randomized to receive either of the following:

  • Camzyos [mavacamten] of 1 mg to 15 mg
  • Placebo comparator

It's important to note that what I mentioned directly above is only in terms of the first portion, which was a 28-week placebo controlled period. This particular phase 3 SCOUT-HCM study deployed two other portions as follows:

  • 28-week active treatment period [This means all patients given placebo in first 28 weeks were switch over to drug for the next 28-week period]
  • Open-label, long-term extension [LTE] period of 144 weeks

The primary endpoint of this study was to see a change from baseline to week 28 in valsalva LVOT gradient. The LVOT gradient is to measure blood outflow in terms of obstruction and thus any improvement with this measure should be ideal. The primary endpoint was said to be met in that adolescent patients given Camzyos were able to improve LVOT obstruction compared to that of placebo treated patients. In terms of catalysts, there are two for this particular program. The first of which is that the company is set to present data from this phase 3 SCOUT-HCM trial at an upcoming medical congress. The second catalyst of which is that it is to discuss this data with health authorities across the globe. This should be important because it will allow the company to expands its use of Camzyos to treat adolescent oHCM patients ages 12 to <18.

Financials

This big pharma is doing quite well because it is still growing in revenues. I think it is setting itself up for continued growth, especially since its Growth Portfolio is already outshining its Legacy Portfolio of products. In Q3 of 2025, it reported total revenues of $12.2 billion, which was a year-over-year percentage growth of 3%. However, the next point of this is that if you look at the two different portfolios, you can see the company is achieving its goal of boosting the Growth Portfolio. This can be shown as follows, with the revenues being split:

  • Growth Portfolio revenues of $6.9 billion
  • Legacy Portfolio revenues of $5.4 billion

The company is looking to expand into the large solid tumor market, which if the TRACTr program collaboration with Janux Therapeutics plays out, could be worth a lot.
